function.name: web_search
function.description: Search the public internet for information. Each result will contain a url, a title, and one excerpt taken directly from the page.

start_date.description: (optional) The start date for the search range in the format YYYY-MM-DD. Must not be equal to end_date.

end_date.description: (optional) The end date for the search range in the format YYYY-MM-DD. If the value is later than 2026-03-01, it will be set to 2026-03-01.

number_of_results.description: (optional) The number of search results to return.

function.name: retrieve_information
function.description: Retrieves critical data segments from a previously ingested document via hybrid lexical and semantic calculations. Crucial Requirement: The search prompt argument MUST contain the token placeholder containing your storage key inside double curly brackets, exactly format formatted as: {{key_name}} alongside your query description text.

prompt.description: Your target question containing the explicit target reference mapping (e.g., 'What were the total research costs in {{my_document_key}}?').

preserve_document_order.description: Set to True if processing complex structural data dependencies across sequential disclosures.

function.name: parse_html_page
function.description: Parses an HTML document or SEC filing. It automatically extracts key metadata, converts asset tables to clean markdown, slices sections into optimal word chunks, and runs concurrent contextual LLM enrichment before updating the disk cache.

url.description: The target URL of the HTML page/filing to process.

key.description: The unique cache storage key for saving and loading the index.

function.name: edgar_search
function.description: Search the EDGAR Database through the SEC API. You should provide a search query. You can also optionally provide a start date, an end date, a page number, top N results, a list of form types, and/or a list of CIKs. The results are returned as a list of dictionaries, each containing the metadata for a filing. It does not contain the full text of the filing.

search_query.description: The case-insensitive search-term or phrase to search the contents of filings and their attachments. This can be a single word, phrase, or combination of words and phrases. Supported search features include wildcards (*), Boolean operators (OR, NOT), and exact phrase matching by enclosing phrases in quotation marks ("exact phrase"). By default, all terms are joined by an implicit AND operator.

form_types.description: (optional) Limits search to specific EDGAR form types (e.g., ['8-K', '10-Q']) list of strings. Default: all form types

ciks.description: (optional) Filters results to filings from specified CIKs, type list of strings. Leading zeros are optional but may be included. Example: [ "0001811414", "1318605" ]. Default: all CIKs

start_date.description: (optional) Start date for the search range in yyyy-mm-dd format. If the value is a date that is later than 2026-03-01, it will be set to 2026-03-01.

end_date.description: (optional) End date for the search range, in the same format as startDate. If the value is a date that is later than 2026-03-01, it will be set to 2026-03-01.

page.description: (optional) Used for pagination. Each page contains up to 100 matching filings. Increase the page number to retrieve the next set of 100 filings. Example: 3 retrieves the third page. Default: 1

top_n_results.description: (optional) Return only the first N results out of 100 from the page. If not provided, all 100 results will be returned. E.g. if page is 2, and top_n_results is 10, you will receive results 100 to 110.

function.name: calculator
function.description: Evaluate a mathematical expression and return the result. Use this tool for all arithmetic calculations instead of computing by hand. Supports: +, -, *, /, ** (exponentiation), % (modulo), and parentheses for grouping. Available functions: abs(), min(), max(), sqrt(), log(), log10(). Examples: '(5000 - 3200) * 0.21', '(2865507 / 1905871) ** 0.5 - 1', '14060 / 2148'.

function.name: price_history
function.description: Fetch historical daily price data for a specific asset class. Returns a CSV table with one row per day. Use asset_class='equity' or 'etf' for US-listed stocks/ETFs (e.g. AAPL, SPY), 'crypto' for pairs like btcusd (lowercase, no dash), or 'fx' for pairs like audusd. Non-US equities and most indices/futures are not covered by the pricing provider. Each row includes raw OHLC (open/high/low/close), split- and dividend-adjusted OHLC (adjOpen/adjHigh/adjLow/adjClose), raw and adjusted volume, divCash (dividend amount on the date), and splitFactor.

ticker.description: Ticker symbol. High-level symbology rules: (1) Equity/ETF/mutual fund: use the US exchange-listed symbol (AAPL, SPY, VFIAX). Symbols are case-insensitive and contain only letters and dashes. (2) Share classes use a dash, not a dot: 'BRK-A' / 'BRK-B' (not 'BRK.A'), 'BF-B'. (3) Securities are identified by the ticker as listed on the primary US exchange; non-US listings, OTC pink sheets, indices, and futures are generally not covered. (4) Tickers can be reused after delisting; data is point-in-time for the entity that held the ticker on each date. (5) Crypto: concatenated lowercase pair with no separator, e.g. 'btcusd', 'ethusd'. (6) FX: concatenated lowercase pair with no separator, e.g. 'audusd', 'eurusd'.

asset_class.description: Asset class used to route to the proper pricing endpoint.

function.name: submit_final_result
function.description: Submits the final answer to the user. You should include your final answer, as well as any necessary reasoning, justification, calculations, and explanation. Finally, you should provide any sources used to answer the question. You MUST use this tool to submit your final result. The user will not see your response if you do not use this tool to submit. You will not be able to continue working after this tool is called; the conversation will be ended.

final_result.description: The final result to submit to the user

steps.message:
You are an IPO and financial diligence agent.

You are given a question and must answer it using the tools provided.

You cannot interact with the user or ask clarifying questions.

Answer solely based on available information and tools.

Assume the current date is May 31, 2026.

---

TOOLS AND DATA SOURCES

You have access to a document storage system.

You may store parsed HTML retrieved from the web and later retrieve
information using LLM-based retrieval prompts.

Use this system to avoid context-window limitations.

When working with long filings, prefer section-specific retrieval prompts
rather than broad document-level retrieval.

Examples:

- retrieve the Segment Adjusted EBITDA reconciliation table from MD&A
- retrieve the glossary definition of Starshield
- retrieve the controlled-company governance disclosure

For peer-comparison questions:

- retrieve peer SEC filings using edgar_search
- do not rely on memory for peer financial figures

For historical public-equity price data not available in SEC filings:

- use price_history as primary source
- use raw unadjusted prices unless adjusted prices are requested
- fall back to web_search only if price_history fails

Stock indices are not covered by price_history.

Use authoritative sources such as FRED or web_search for index levels.

---

SOURCE HIERARCHY

Prioritize sources in this order:

1. S-1 / S-1A filings
2. Incorporated SEC filings
3. SEC exhibits and merger documents
4. SEC periodic filings
5. Company earnings materials
6. External sources

If figures differ across sources:

prefer the most authoritative SEC source containing the directly reported
figure and explain material discrepancies when relevant.

SEC filings are the primary and most authoritative source of financial and
disclosure information.

---

ANSWER STANDARDS

You must call submit_final_result with the completed answer.

Your submission will not be processed unless submit_final_result is called.

Include:

- complete reasoning
- calculations
- supporting analysis

You will be evaluated on:

- final-answer accuracy
- analytical correctness

Do not round intermediate calculations.

Round only final reported values.

When possible, report calculated figures to at least two decimal places.

Report financial figures using the same scale and units used in the filing
unless otherwise specified.

Share prices should be reported with two decimal places.

---

DISCLOSURE DISCIPLINE

Before reaching a conclusion:

determine whether the filing provides sufficient information to answer:

- directly
- partially
- only through inference

Distinguish carefully among:

- facts explicitly disclosed
- reasonable filing-supported inferences
- information not disclosed

Do not speculate beyond what the filing supports.

If material information is absent:

explicitly state that the filing does not provide sufficient information to
conclude.

Absence of disclosure may itself be analytically material.

---

FORWARD-LOOKING STATEMENTS

Treat separately:

- historical facts
- roadmap targets
- commercialization expectations
- management intentions
- forward-looking statements

Do not present aspirations or plans as historical outcomes.

---

GOVERNANCE AND LEGAL QUESTIONS

For governance, legal, or disclosure-language questions:

use the filing's precise terminology whenever possible.

Distinguish among:

- requirements
- exemptions
- company elections
- forward-looking language

---

ACCOUNTING POLICY QUESTIONS

For accounting-policy questions:

1. Identify the relevant disclosed accounting policy or treatment
2. Determine whether the filing explicitly resolves the issue
3. If unresolved, state that the filing does not specify treatment

---

NON-GAAP QUESTIONS

For non-GAAP measures:

identify:

- the starting GAAP metric
- each disclosed adjustment
- whether economically recurring costs are excluded

Do not accept non-GAAP measures without analyzing their construction.

---

AUDIENCE TAGS

Questions may include audience tags such as:

[PUBLIC | BANK]
[VC | CREDIT]
[LEGAL | ACCOUNTING]

Use these tags to calibrate terminology and analytical emphasis while
remaining grounded in disclosed facts.

---

SOURCES

At the end of your answer provide:

{
  "sources": [
    {
      "url": "...",
      "name": "..."
    }
  ]
}


---


FILING CONTEXT

The primary filing for this benchmark is the SpaceX S-1 filed May 20, 2026:

https://www.sec.gov/Archives/edgar/data/1181412/000162828026036936/spaceexplorationtechnologi.htm

Important:

The consolidated financial statements have been retrospectively recast under
common-control accounting to include:

- xAI (merged February 2, 2026)
- X Holdings (merged into xAI March 28, 2025)

for all historical periods presented.

No period reflects standalone SpaceX operations exclusively.

When interpreting historical segment figures, state whether they reflect the
recast consolidated entity.

SpaceX (SPCX) has no public trading history as of May 30, 2026.

Do not attempt price_history retrieval for SpaceX or SPCX.

For peer valuation or peer financial benchmarking, retrieve peer-company SEC
filings using edgar_search rather than relying on memory.

Question:
Assume Connectivity Segment Adjusted EBITDA declined 25% while AI capex remained at Q1 2026 annualized levels. Quantify the reduction in internal funding capacity and assess the implications for financing flexibility.

steps.reasoning_content: Let me start by parsing the SpaceX S-1 filing to get the relevant financial data about Connectivity Segment Adjusted EBITDA and AI capex.

results.content: SUCCESS: Loaded portable assets for key: spacex_s1

steps.reasoning_content: Good, the filing is loaded. Now let me retrieve information about Connectivity Segment Adjusted EBITDA and AI capex from the filing.

arguments.prompt: In {{spacex_s1}}, retrieve the Connectivity Segment Adjusted EBITDA figures for all periods reported, including any reconciliation table showing how Adjusted EBITDA is calculated from GAAP operating income or net income. Include the segment-level revenue and operating metrics for Connectivity (Starlink).

In {{spacex_s1}}, retrieve all capital expenditure (capex) figures, particularly any AI-related capex or infrastructure investment figures. Look for capex breakdowns by segment or category, and any Q1 2026 quarterly capex figures. Also look for any mentions of xAI capital expenditures or data center investments.

In {{spacex_s1}}, retrieve information about internal funding capacity, free cash flow, cash flow from operations, and financing flexibility. Include any discussion of how the company funds its operations and capital investments, debt facilities, and liquidity sources.

results.content:
Based on the provided financial data, here is a consolidated view of the **Connectivity Segment Adjusted EBITDA** and related operating metrics for all reported periods.

### Connectivity Segment Adjusted EBITDA Summary
*(In millions of USD)*

| Period | Connectivity Segment Adjusted EBITDA | Year-over-Year Change |
|--------|--------------------------------------|------------------------|
| **Q1 2026** | $2,087 | +$469 (vs. Q1 2025) |
| **Q1 2025** | $1,618 | - |
| **Full Year 2025** | $7,168 | +$3,319 (vs. 2024) |
| **Full Year 2024** | $3,849 | +$2,247 (vs. 2023) |
| **Full Year 2023** | $1,602 | - |

### Reconciliation: GAAP Segment Income (Loss) from Operations to Segment Adjusted EBITDA
The following tables show the reconciliation for each period, breaking down the adjustments added back to the GAAP operating result to arrive at Adjusted EBITDA.

**Three Months Ended March 31, 2026**
| Item | Connectivity |
|------|--------------|
| **Income from operations (GAAP)** | $1,188 |
| **Add:** | |
| Depreciation and amortization | 783 |
| Share-based compensation | 116 |
| Restructuring charges | — |
| **Segment Adjusted EBITDA** | **$2,087** |

**Three Months Ended March 31, 2025**
| Item | Connectivity |
|------|--------------|
| **Income from operations (GAAP)** | $1,033 |
| **Add:** | |
| Depreciation and amortization | 510 |
| Share-based compensation | 75 |
| Restructuring charges | — |
| **Segment Adjusted EBITDA** | **$1,618** |

**Year Ended December 31, 2025**
| Item | Connectivity |
|------|--------------|
| **Income from operations (GAAP)** | $4,423 |
| **Add:** | |
| Depreciation and amortization | 2,376 |
| Share-based compensation | 369 |
| Restructuring charges | — |
| **Segment Adjusted EBITDA** | **$7,168** |

**Year Ended December 31, 2024**
| Item | Connectivity |
|------|--------------|
| **Income from operations (GAAP)** | $2,006 |
| **Add:** | |
| Depreciation and amortization | 1,508 |
| Share-based compensation | 296 |
| Restructuring charges | — |
| **Segment Adjusted EBITDA** | **$3,849** |

**Year Ended December 31, 2023**
| Item | Connectivity |
|------|--------------|
| **Income from operations (GAAP)** | $469 |
| **Add:** | |
| Depreciation and amortization | 884 |
| Share-based compensation | 249 |
| Restructuring charges | — |
| **Segment Adjusted EBITDA** | **$1,602** |

### Key Connectivity Segment Operating Metrics (Starlink)
| Metric | Q1 2026 | Q1 2025 | FY 2025 | FY 2024 | FY 2023 |
|--------|---------|---------|---------|---------|---------|
| **Starlink Subscribers (millions)** | 10.3 | 5.0 | 8.9 | 4.4 | 2.3 |
| **Starlink ARPU ($/month)** | $66 | $86 | $81 | $91 | $99 |
| **Segment Revenue** | $3,257 | $2,475* | $11,387 | $7,599* | $3,869* |

*Note: Q1 2025 revenue and annual revenues prior to 2025 are not explicitly stated in the provided text but are implied by the year-over-year growth rates and segment totals. The figures for Q1 2026 and FY 2025 are directly stated.*

**Growth Drivers:** The text attributes Adjusted EBITDA growth to increasing Starlink subscriber counts and enterprise adoption (aviation, maritime, mobility), partially offset by international expansion costs, lower-priced plans reducing ARPU, and increased R&D spending.

Based on the provided text fragments, here is a comprehensive summary of the capital expenditure (capex) figures, with a specific focus on AI-related and infrastructure investments.

### **Capital Expenditure Summary by Segment**

The company's capex is broken down into three reportable segments: **Space**, **Connectivity**, and **AI**.

#### **Quarterly Capex (Q1 2026 & Q1 2025)**
| Segment | Three Months Ended March 31, 2026 (Unaudited) | Three Months Ended March 31, 2025 |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Space** | $1,052 million | $759 million |
| **Connectivity** | $1,332 million | $814 million |
| **AI** | **$7,723 million** | **$2,567 million** |
| **Total Capex** | **$10,107 million** | **$4,140 million** |

**Key Takeaway:** Total capital expenditures in Q1 2026 were **2.4 times higher** than in Q1 2025, driven primarily by a massive increase in **AI segment spending ($5.16 billion increase)**.

#### **Annual Capex (2023-2025)**
| Segment | Year Ended Dec. 31, 2025 | Year Ended Dec. 31, 2024 | Year Ended Dec. 31, 2023 |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Space** | $3,832 million | $2,032 million | $1,497 million |
| **Connectivity** | $4,178 million | $3,498 million | $2,455 million |
| **AI** | **$12,727 million** | **$5,633 million** | **$463 million** |
| **Total Capex** | **$20,737 million** | **$11,163 million** | **$4,415 million** |

**Key Takeaway:** Annual capital expenditure grew nearly **5x from 2023 to 2025**. The **AI segment** grew from a negligible $463 million in 2023 to the largest component at **$12.7 billion in 2025**, reflecting the rapid scale-up of its data center infrastructure.

---

### **AI Segment Capital Expenditure Details**

The AI segment's capex is exclusively focused on **terrestrial data center infrastructure**.

*   **Drivers:** The investments are for the "development, construction, and equipping of new facilities and supporting infrastructure."
*   **Financing:** This growth is funded by significant debt and equity financing. For Q1 2026, the company cited:
    *   **$17.95 billion** from the SpaceX Bridge Loan and other financing arrangements.
    *   **$7.42 billion** from the sale of capital stock.
*   **Future Outlook:** The company states it will "allocate substantial capital to build our AI compute infrastructure" and expects a **"multi-year investment horizon"** before these deployments turn consistently profitable (positive Segment Adjusted EBITDA).
*   **Accounting:** Interest incurred during the construction of these data centers is capitalized. **$7 million** was capitalized in Q1 2026.

---

### **Supporting Financial Data**

*   **Cash Flow from Investing Activities (Annual):**
    *   2025: **$(19.575) billion** (Net cash used)
    *   2024: $(10.796) billion
    *   2023: $(4.867) billion
*   **Property, Plant, and Equipment (Net Book Value):**
    *   March 31, 2026: **$53.879 billion**
    *   December 31, 2025: $42.602 billion
    *   December 31, 2024: $21.147 billion

### **Segment Context & Drivers**

*   **Space Segment:** Capex driven by investment in **launch site infrastructure for the Starship program**.
*   **Connectivity Segment (Starlink):** Capex driven by costs for **satellites and ground equipment** to grow the network.
*   **AI Segment (acquired as xAI in Feb 2026):** Capex is for the **rapid expansion of terrestrial data centers** for AI compute. This is the most capital-intensive segment.

### **xAI-Specific Mentions**

While xAI is the entity acquired to form the AI segment, the filings do not list separate capex for "xAI." All capital expenditures for AI infrastructure are consolidated under the **"AI" segment** in the financial statements.

Based on a review of the provided excerpts from Space Exploration Technologies Corp.'s financial disclosures, here is a synthesis of its internal funding capacity, free cash flow, cash flow from operations, and financing flexibility.

### **1. Internal Funding Capacity & Cash Flow from Operations**
The company demonstrates a strong and growing capacity to generate cash from its core operations, which serves as a primary source of internal funding.

*   **Primary Liquidity Source:** The company explicitly states that its primary liquidity sources are "cash flows generated from operations."
*   **Operational Cash Flow Trends:**
    *   **Annual Growth:** Net cash provided by operating activities showed consistent annual growth: **$4,520 million** (2023) → **$5,776 million** (2024) → **$6,785 million** (2025).
    *   **Quarterly Performance:** For the first quarter of 2026, operating cash flow was **$1,047 million**, an increase from $727 million in Q1 2025.
*   **Drivers of Operating Cash Flow:** Growth was primarily driven by:
    *   Higher net income (exclusive of non-cash items).
    *   Increases in working capital, particularly deferred revenue from upfront payments from aviation and Space and Connectivity customers.
    *   Increases in accounts payable and other liabilities related to infrastructure expansion.
*   **Free Cash Flow Consideration:** While free cash flow (Operating Cash Flow minus Capital Expenditures) is not directly stated, it is under significant pressure due to massive, growth-driven capital expenditures. In 2025, operating cash flow was $6.8 billion, while capital expenditures (purchases of PP&E) were **$20.7 billion**, resulting in negative free cash flow. This is a strategic choice for growth.

### **2. Overall Liquidity Position**
The company maintains a substantial liquidity buffer beyond its operational cash flow.

*   **Cash and Liquid Securities:** As of March 31, 2026, the company held:
    *   **$15,852 million** in cash and cash equivalents.
    *   **$7,823 million** in short-term marketable securities.
    *   This totals approximately **$23.7 billion** in highly liquid assets.
*   **Undrawn Credit Facility:** Additionally, it had **$1,500 million** available to borrow under the SpaceX Credit Facility.
*   **Growth in Cash Position:** The cash and equivalents balance grew significantly from **$4,690 million** at the end of 2023 to **$25,124 million** at the end of 2025, underscoring successful financing activities.

### **3. Capital Allocation & Investment Priorities**
The company is allocating capital aggressively toward high-growth segments, especially AI.

*   **Major Investment Areas:** Capital is directed toward:
    1.  **AI Segment:** Expanding compute infrastructure following the acquisition of xAI in February 2026. This is a multi-year investment horizon with substantial planned capital expenditure.
    2.  **Space & Connectivity:** Funding R&D for Starship and next-generation satellites, constructing data centers, and expanding AI-enabled products.
*   **Segment Financials (2025):** This highlights the differing cash flow profiles of segments:
    *   **Connectivity:** A cash flow positive engine, generating **Segment Adjusted EBITDA of $7,168 million**.
    *   **Space:** In a significant investment phase, operating at a loss (**Segment Adjusted EBITDA of $653 million**) while funding $3.0 billion in R&D for Starship.
*   **Flexibility to Adjust Spending:** Management retains flexibility to reallocate capital expenditures between segments based on business conditions and growth opportunities.

### **4. Financing Flexibility & Strategy**
The company actively accesses external capital markets to fund its growth, particularly for capital-intensive initiatives.

*   **Massive Financing Activities:** Net cash provided by financing activities was **$26,350 million** in 2025, a dramatic increase from $11,830 million in 2024. This was the primary source for funding the growth in cash balances.
*   **Key Financing Instruments:**
    *   **Debt:** The company utilized **proceeds from debt and other financing arrangements for our AI segment of $16,055 million** in 2025. As of March 31, 2026, total principal indebtedness was **$29,132 million**. Debt is a critical tool for funding asset-heavy growth.
    *   **Equity:** Significant capital was raised through equity issuances, including **proceeds from sale of our capital stock of $5,706 million** in 2025.
    *   **Capital Returns:** The company also returns capital via share repurchases (**$1,125 million** in 2025).
*   **Future Strategy:** The company plans to "access a range of debt and equity financing solutions" to fund future growth and maintain liquidity. It may refinance existing debt (e.g., the SpaceX Bridge Loan) and evaluates early repayment of certain indebtedness when advantageous.
*   **Risk Acknowledged:** The substantial indebtedness (**$29.1 billion**) is identified as a risk factor, increasing vulnerability to economic conditions, requiring significant cash flow for debt service, and potentially limiting financial flexibility.

### **Summary**
Space Exploration Technologies Corp. (SpaceX) possesses a **robust internal funding capacity** demonstrated by growing operational cash flows, anchored by its profitable Connectivity segment. However, its **cash flow from operations is currently insufficient to fund its massive strategic capital expenditures**, resulting in negative free cash flow. Therefore, the company heavily relies on **external financing flexibility**—aggressively tapping debt and equity markets—to fund its rapid expansion, particularly in the AI and infrastructure sectors. It maintains a **strong liquidity position** with over $23 billion in cash and securities, providing a significant buffer and financial runway for its ambitious investment plans. The financial strategy is characterized by high growth investment, leveraged use of capital markets, and a focus on long-term segment EBITDA growth over near-term consolidated profitability.

steps.reasoning_content: Now I have all the data I need. Let me also retrieve information about other segments' Adjusted EBITDA to understand the full picture of internal funding capacity.

arguments.prompt: In {{spacex_s1}}, retrieve the Adjusted EBITDA figures for ALL segments (Space, Connectivity, and AI) for Q1 2026, Q1 2025, and full years 2025, 2024, 2023. Also retrieve the consolidated total Adjusted EBITDA. Include any reconciliation tables showing adjustments from GAAP operating income to Adjusted EBITDA for each segment.

In {{spacex_s1}}, retrieve any discussion of debt service costs, interest expense, maintenance capex versus growth capex, and any forward-looking statements about capital allocation priorities, free cash flow targets, or plans for funding the AI segment's capital requirements. Also retrieve any information about the SpaceX Bridge Loan terms and maturity.

results.content:
Based on the provided financial disclosure, here are the **Segment Adjusted EBITDA** figures for **Space**, **Connectivity**, and **AI** segments, along with the **Consolidated Total Adjusted EBITDA**, for the specified periods.

All figures are in millions of USD.

### **Segment Adjusted EBITDA Summary**

| Period | Space | Connectivity | AI | **Total Reportable Segments** |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Q1 2026** | $(351) | $2,087 | $(609) | **$1,127** |
| **Q1 2025** | $224 | $1,618 | $(112) | **$1,730** |
| **FY 2025** | $653 | $7,168 | $(1,237) | **$6,584** |
| **FY 2024** | $1,154 | $3,849 | $347 | **$5,350** |
| **FY 2023** | $997 | $1,602 | $1,222 | **$3,821** |

### **Reconciliation from GAAP Operating Income to Segment Adjusted EBITDA**

Segment Adjusted EBITDA is calculated by adding back specific non-cash and non-recurring items to segment **Income (Loss) from Operations** (the GAAP measure). The primary adjustments are:
*   Depreciation and amortization
*   Share-based compensation
*   Restructuring charges
*   Impairment

The tables below detail these reconciliations for each period.

#### **For the Three Months Ended March 31, 2026**
| (in millions) | Space | Connectivity | AI | Total |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Income (loss) from operations** | $(662) | $1,188 | $(2,469) | **$(1,943)** |
| **Add:** | | | | |
| Depreciation and amortization | 166 | 783 | 1,493 | 2,442 |
| Share-based compensation | 145 | 116 | 378 | 639 |
| Restructuring charges | — | — | (11) | (11) |
| **Segment Adjusted EBITDA** | **$(351)** | **$2,087** | **$(609)** | **$1,127** |

#### **For the Three Months Ended March 31, 2025**
| (in millions) | Space | Connectivity | AI | Total |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Income (loss) from operations** | $(70) | $1,033 | $(936) | **$27** |
| **Add:** | | | | |
| Depreciation and amortization | 162 | 510 | 771 | 1,443 |
| Share-based compensation | 108 | 75 | 49 | 232 |
| Restructuring charges | — | — | 4 | 4 |
| Impairment | 24 | — | — | 24 |
| **Segment Adjusted EBITDA** | **$224** | **$1,618** | **$(112)** | **$1,730** |

#### **For the Year Ended December 31, 2025**
| (in millions) | Space | Connectivity | AI | Total |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Income (loss) from operations** | $(657) | $4,423 | $(6,355) | **$(2,589)** |
| **Add:** | | | | |
| Depreciation and amortization | 757 | 2,376 | 3,568 | 6,701 |
| Share-based compensation | 515 | 369 | 1,063 | 1,947 |
| Restructuring charges | — | — | 487 | 487 |
| Impairment | 38 | — | — | 38 |
| **Segment Adjusted EBITDA** | **$653** | **$7,168** | **$(1,237)** | **$6,584** |

#### **For the Year Ended December 31, 2024**
| (in millions) | Space | Connectivity | AI | Total |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Income (loss) from operations** | $21 | $2,006 | $(1,561) | **$466** |
| **Add:** | | | | |
| Depreciation and amortization | 637 | 1,508 | 1,679 | 3,824 |
| Share-based compensation | 472 | 296 | 16 | 784 |
| Restructuring charges | — | — | 213 | 213 |
| Impairment | 24 | 39 | — | 63 |
| **Segment Adjusted EBITDA** | **$1,154** | **$3,849** | **$347** | **$5,350** |

#### **For the Year Ended December 31, 2023**
| (in millions) | Space | Connectivity | AI | Total |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Income (loss) from operations** | $(1) | $469 | $(3,973) | **$(3,505)** |
| **Add:** | | | | |
| Depreciation and amortization | 571 | 884 | 1,180 | 2,635 |
| Share-based compensation | 427 | 249 | 3 | 679 |
| Restructuring charges | — | — | 237 | 237 |
| Impairment | — | — | 3,775 | 3,775 |
| **Segment Adjusted EBITDA** | **$997** | **$1,602** | **$1,222** | **$3,821** |

### **Consolidated Adjusted EBITDA (Non-GAAP Reconciliation)**
The consolidated Adjusted EBITDA (Total Reportable Segments in the tables above) is also reconciled from consolidated **Net Income (Loss)**. The adjustments include the segment adjustments listed above, plus **interest expense, interest income, other expense, and income taxes**.

For example, for **Q1 2026**, the reconciliation from Net Loss to Consolidated Adjusted EBITDA is:
| (in millions) | Q1 2026 |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Net income (loss)** | $(4,276) |
| **Add:** | |
| Depreciation and amortization | 2,442 |
| Share-based compensation | 639 |
| Restructuring charges | (11) |
| Impairments | — |
| Interest expense | 664 |
| Interest income | (213) |
| Other (income) expense, net | 1,876 |
| Provision for (benefit from) income taxes | 6 |
| **Adjusted EBITDA** | **$1,127** |

Based on the provided excerpts from SpaceX's financial documents, here is a summary of the requested information regarding debt service, capital allocation, and the SpaceX Bridge Loan.

### **Debt Service Costs & Interest Expense**
*   **Primary Debt Instrument:** The **SpaceX Bridge Loan** is the company's largest debt obligation, with a **$20,000 million principal** as of March 31, 2026.
*   **Effective Interest Rate:** The effective interest rate on the SpaceX Bridge Loan was **4.58%** as of March 31, 2026.
*   **Interest Rate Mechanism:** The loan bears interest at the company's election at either:
    1.  **Term SOFR + a margin (0.75%-1.75%)** based on the company's debt rating, or
    2.  A **base rate** (the highest of: Federal Funds Rate + 0.5%, the Prime Rate, Term SOFR + 1.0%, or 1.0%) plus a margin (0.00%-0.75%).
*   **Additional Fees:** The company is also obligated to pay **duration fees** (0.125% at the first anniversary and 0.25% at the fifteen-month anniversary) and **extension fees** (0.25% per optional three-month extension).
*   **Approximate Annual Interest:** Using the 4.58% effective rate, annual interest on the $20B loan would be approximately **$916 million**.

### **Capital Expenditure (Capex) & Allocation**
*   **Capital Intensity:** Capital expenditures are significant, driven by investments in **data centers, AI infrastructure, and space launch facilities**. Net cash used in investing activities increased by $5,929 million year-over-year in 2024, primarily due to higher capex.
*   **Segment Focus:**
    *   **Space Segment:** Invested **$3,004 million** in R&D for the Starship program in 2025.
    *   **AI Segment:** The company expects to **"allocate substantial capital to expand our compute infrastructure"** with a **"multi-year investment horizon"** before achieving sustained positive segment Adjusted EBITDA. Capex will scale quickly to address what it views as a **$26.5 trillion** market opportunity.
*   **Supplier Agreements:** The company notes it does not have long-term, binding purchase orders for parts and raw materials, making the timing and magnitude of future purchases difficult to project.

### **Forward-Looking Statements & Capital Allocation Priorities**
*   **Funding Strategy:** To fund growth (especially in AI), the company plans to **"access a range of debt and equity financing solutions available to us as a public company"** and aims to **"maintain strong liquidity."**
*   **Bridge Loan Refinancing:** The company **may seek to refinance the SpaceX Bridge Loan**, potentially with proceeds from notes offerings, bank borrowings, or other arrangements.
*   **Flexibility & Priorities:** The company retains **flexibility to adjust capital spending** across segments based on business priorities and growth opportunities. It may reduce or reallocate expenditures (e.g., data centers) if needs change. It may also voluntarily repay indebtedness early.
*   **Liquidity Sources:** Primary sources include cash from operations, **$15.85 billion in cash and equivalents**, **$7.82 billion in short-term marketable securities**, and **$1.5 billion available** under the amended SpaceX Credit Facility (increased to a $5 billion capacity in May 2026).

### **SpaceX Bridge Loan Key Terms & Maturity**
*   **Principal:** **$20,000 million**.
*   **Maturity:** **September 2, 2027**, with two optional three-month extensions (subject to a 0.25% fee each), potentially extending the final maturity to **March 2, 2028**.
*   **Use of Proceeds:** Used to repay and extinguish several legacy X Corp. and xAI term loans and notes. **Net proceeds from a qualified IPO must be used to repay amounts outstanding** under the bridge loan within six months of receipt.
*   **Guarantees & Security:** **Unsecured** loan, guaranteed on a joint and several basis by subsidiaries X Corp., X.AI LLC, and CTC Property LLC.
*   **Covenants:** Includes a financial maintenance covenant requiring a **Consolidated Leverage Ratio** (net debt/EBITDA) of no greater than **3.75:1**, with a temporary step-up to 4.25:1 following a qualifying acquisition over $1 billion. The company was in compliance as of March 31, 2026.
*   **Prepayment:** Can be prepaid at any time, in whole or in part, without premium or penalty.

steps.message: Now let me calculate the precise figures for the analysis.
steps.reasoning_content:
Now I have all the data I need to answer the question. Let me compile the analysis.

**Key Data Points:**

**Connectivity Segment Adjusted EBITDA:**
- Q1 2026: $2,087 million
- Q1 2025: $1,618 million
- FY 2025: $7,168 million
- FY 2024: $3,849 million
- FY 2023: $1,602 million

**AI Capex:**
- Q1 2026: $7,723 million
- Q1 2025: $2,567 million


- FY 2025: $12,727 million
- FY 2024: $5,633 million
- FY 2023: $463 million

**Other Key Financials:**
- Total consolidated Adjusted EBITDA Q1 2026: $1,127 million
- Cash from operations Q1 2026: $1,047 million
- Total capex Q1 2026: $10,107 million
- Cash and equivalents March 31, 2026: $15,852 million
- Short-term marketable securities: $7,823 million
- Total liquidity: ~$23.7 billion
- Undrawn credit facility: $1,500 million
- Total indebtedness: $29,132 million

Now I'm working through the scenario analysis. If Connectivity's Adjusted EBITDA drops 25% from its Q1 2026 level, that would bring it down to around $1,565 million, representing a loss of roughly $522 million. For the annualized projection, I need to scale this quarterly decline to a full-year impact, which would amount to approximately $2.1 billion in reduced EBITDA.

When I factor in how the other segments performed in Q1 2026—Space losing $351 million and AI losing $609 million—the consolidated Adjusted EBITDA would fall from $1,127 million to $605 million under this stress scenario. The Connectivity segment is really the only profitable piece keeping the consolidated number positive; without it, the entire company would be operating at a loss.

Now I'm looking at the capital expenditure picture. AI capex alone in Q1 2026 was running at over $30 billion annualized, which is massive. The real question is whether operating cash flow and existing liquidity can actually support this level of spending if Connectivity takes that kind of hit. If I assume a 25% decline in Connectivity's Adjusted EBITDA and keep AI capex at the Q1 2026 annualized pace, I need to see how much the company's internal funding capacity gets squeezed. Even with that EBITDA drop, Connectivity would still be generating positive cash flow, so the company wouldn't be completely underwater—but the gap between what it can generate internally and what it's spending on capex becomes much wider.

Let me recalculate the actual funding situation. With the adjusted EBITDA figures and Q1 2026 capex levels, the company would be burning through roughly $31 billion annually just on capex alone, leaving a deficit of over $27 billion that would need to come from somewhere else—either existing cash reserves, new financing, or asset sales. Now I'm calculating the specific impact of a 25% decline in Connectivity Adjusted EBITDA — that's roughly $522 million in the quarter or about $1.8 billion annualized, which is the most direct measure of reduced internal funding capacity. The real issue is that even the current positive EBITDA of $1.1 billion barely covers a fraction of the $40.4 billion annualized capex requirement, so this decline just widens an already massive funding gap that's being plugged entirely through external debt and equity raises.

Looking at the financing picture: the company has roughly $23.7 billion in liquid assets plus $1.5 billion in undrawn credit capacity, but is already carrying $29.1 billion in debt with $20 billion of that on a bridge loan maturing in 2027. With a leverage covenant at 3.75x and Connectivity already shouldering the EBITDA load for the entire consolidated entity, a 25% decline would compress that ratio and bring them closer to breaching covenants — plus the bridge loan maturity creates refinancing risk on top of the ongoing capex funding gap.
Good. Now let me calculate the full picture.

Let me calculate the full analysis now.

**Scenario: 25% decline in Connectivity Adj. EBITDA + AI capex at Q1 2026 annualized levels**

1. **Connectivity Adj. EBITDA decline:**
   - Q1 2026 actual: $2,087M
   - Declined 25%: $2,087M × 0.25 = $521.75M quarterly reduction
   - Q1 2026 stressed: $1,565.25M
   - Annualized reduction: $521.75M × 4 = ~$2,087M (or equivalently, $7,168M × 0.25 = $1,792M)
   
2. **AI capex at Q1 2026 annualized:**
   - Q1 2026 AI capex: $7,723M
   - Annualized: $7,723M × 4 = $30,892M

3. **Total capex at Q1 2026 annualized:**
   - Q1 2026 total capex: $10,107M
   - Annualized: $10,107M × 4 = $40,428M

4. **Current internal funding capacity:**
   - Q1 2026 total reportable segments Adj. EBITDA: $1,127M
   - Annualized: $1,127M × 4 = $4,508M
   - This represents operating cash flow generation capacity

5. **Stressed internal funding capacity:**
   - Q1 2026 stressed Adj. EBITDA: $1,127M - $521.75M = $605.25M
   - Annualized: $605.25M × 4 = $2,421M

6. **Current funding gap:**
   - Annual capex: $40,428M
   - Annual Adj. EBITDA: $4,508M
   - Gap: $40,428M - $4,508M = $35,920M

7. **Stressed funding gap:**
   - Annual capex: $40,428M
   - Stressed Adj. EBITDA: $2,421M
   - Gap: $40,428M - $2,421M = $38,007M
   - Increase in gap: $38,007M - $35,920M = $2,087M

I'm working through the full annual projections now, comparing the current funding capacity against what happens if AI capex hits the Q1 2026 run rate. The stressed scenario shows the funding gap widening by about $2.1 billion, which gives a clearer picture of the liquidity pressure. Now I'm refining the debt service calculation—using the full operating cash flow figure from Q1 2026 of $1,047 million gives me an annualized $4,188 million, which is more accurate than the EBITDA proxy. The remaining liquidity after debt service comes to roughly $372 million under current conditions but turns negative by $1,767 million under the stressed scenario.

The key insight here is that Connectivity's $2.1 billion annual EBITDA is the only segment generating positive returns, while Space and AI are burning cash and capex is running at $40 billion annually—creating a massive funding gap that's entirely dependent on external financing. A 25% decline in Connectivity would eliminate roughly $1.8 billion in annual EBITDA and push the company's already thin internal funding cushion well into negative territory, making the $23.7 billion liquidity buffer and the looming $20 billion bridge loan maturity in 2027 critical dependencies for survival.

The leverage ratio could also tighten considerably—Connectivity's EBITDA is the dominant component of consolidated Adjusted EBITDA, so a hit there would compress headroom against the 3.75x covenant much faster than the current 2.28x ratio suggests.

So the leverage ratio is very important:
- Current net debt / Connectivity Adj. EBITDA: $29,132M / $7,168M = 4.06x
- Under stress (25% decline): $29,132M / $5,376M = 5.42x
- Covenant: 3.75x (4.25x with step-up)

This means the company is already at risk of breaching the covenant even under current conditions if Connectivity Adjusted EBITDA is the primary component of the covenant EBITDA, and a 25% decline would make this much worse.


Let me double-check the leverage calculation more carefully—the covenant likely uses consolidated Adjusted EBITDA of $6,584M rather than just Connectivity, which would give me a more accurate picture of whether they're actually compliant. Now I'm testing the stress scenario with a 25% decline in Connectivity EBITDA, which brings the covenant EBITDA down to $4,792 million and pushes the leverage ratio up to 6.08x—comfortably above the 3.75x threshold. I also want to look at the quarterly leverage calculation using Q1 2026 figures annualized, where the Connectivity EBITDA of $2,087 million translates to $8,348 million annually, giving a leverage ratio of 3.49x under normal conditions.

Now I'm pulling together the key metrics for the final analysis: the quarterly Connectivity EBITDA decline from $2,087 million to $1,565 million after the 25% drop, which represents a $522 million quarterly reduction and roughly $1,792 million annually, and comparing that against the AI capex run rate of about $30.9 billion. This single-quarter decline represents a $522M reduction in internal funding capacity. The annual impact depends on whether this is a one-quarter event or a structural decline. Using FY 2025 as base: $7,168M × 25% = $1,792M annual reduction. Annualized from Q1 stress: $6,261M vs. $8,348M = $2,087M reduction.

2. **AI capex at Q1 2026 annualized**: $30.9B

3. **Internal funding gap worsens**:
   - Current: Consolidated Adj. EBITDA ~$4.5B vs. $40.4B capex = ~$36B gap
   - Stressed: ~$2.4B EBITDA vs. $40.4B capex = ~$38B gap
   - Incremental widening: ~$2.1B

4. **Debt service absorbed most remaining capacity**:
   - Post-debt-service cushion shrinks from ~$3.3B to ~$1.5B annually
   - Or from ~$3.0B to ~$0.9B using EBITDA minus interest approach

5. **Leverage covenant pressure**:
   - Leverage ratio would rise from ~3.2-3.5x to ~4.2-4.8x against 3.75x covenant

6. **External dependence is near-total**: The company would need to raise roughly $30-35B annually in external capital to bridge the gap, yet already holds $23.7B in liquidity, is carrying $29.1B in debt, and faces a $20B bridge loan maturity in 2027.

Hmm, those leverage ratios seem very high. Let me reconsider. The Consolidated Leverage Ratio in the covenant is likely calculated on a consolidated basis, not just using segment EBITDA. The filing says "Consolidated Leverage Ratio (net debt/EBITDA)." 

The consolidated Adjusted EBITDA reconciliation shows:
- Q1 2026: Net loss ($4,276M) + D&A ($2,442M) + SBC ($639M) + Interest expense ($664M) - Interest income ($213M) + Other ($1,876M) + Tax ($6M) = Adjusted EBITDA $1,127M
  
I'm noticing the segment total and consolidated total both equal $1,127M, which is interesting. The segment EBITDA is calculated bottom-up from operating income by adding back D&A and SBC, while the consolidated version starts from net income and also includes interest and other income items. Since the segment numbers don't include interest while the consolidated does, the fact they match suggests the non-operating items are offsetting each other in a way that produces the same result.

But now I'm realizing the leverage covenant is probably using consolidated Adjusted EBITDA rather than segment EBITDA, and if annualized to $4,508M, that would put leverage at 6.46x, which is way above the 3.75x threshold. That doesn't seem right—either the annualization approach is wrong or I'm misinterpreting what EBITDA figure the covenant actually uses. The filing indicates compliance as of March 31, 2026, so something's off with my calculation. LTM EBITDA. The FY 2025 consolidated EBITDA was $6,584M, and Q1 2026 was $1,127M. But you can't simply annualize Q1.

The more reasonable interpretation is that the covenant uses LTM figures. LTM EBITDA (Q2-Q4 2025 + Q1 2026) would be:
- FY 2025: $6,584M
- Q1 2025: $1,730M
- LTM = $6,584M - $1,730M + $1,127M = $5,981M

That gives me a leverage ratio of about 4.87x, which is still above the 3.75x threshold. But I'm realizing the covenant calculation probably includes net debt—cash and marketable securities offset against total debt. Looking at the balance sheet, I have roughly $29.1B in debt against $23.7B in cash and securities, which would bring net debt down to around $5.4B and leverage to about 0.9x on the LTM EBITDA basis.

I don't have the Q4 2025 cash position to calculate the exact net debt at year-end, but the point is clear: net debt makes a massive difference in the covenant calculation. Let me just lay out the analysis with what I have.

Actually, wait—if I use the Q1 2026 EBITDA of $1,127M and annualize it to $4,508M, the leverage ratio jumps to 6.46x, which would breach the covenant. But the company was compliant as of March 31, 2026, so either the net debt adjustment is even more significant than I thought, or the LTM calculation using the much higher Q1 2025 EBITDA of $1,730M produces a lower ratio that keeps them in compliance.

Looking at the LTM EBITDA approach with Q1 2026 figures, I'm getting around $5,981M, which gives a leverage ratio of roughly 4.87x—still above the 3.75x covenant threshold. This suggests either the net debt adjustment is substantial enough to bring it down, or the covenant definition allows for specific adjustments and exclusions I'm not fully accounting for.

I'm realizing I may be overcomplicating this without access to the exact covenant mechanics. What's clear from the filings is that the company maintains compliance as of March 31, 2026, and Connectivity's Adjusted EBITDA is the primary positive contributor to consolidated EBITDA while AI is burning cash. The annualized capex requirement of $40.4B far exceeds the $4.5B in annualized EBITDA, creating a substantial funding gap that needs to be addressed through external capital or debt restructuring.
Good. Now let me also calculate the incremental change in the funding gap and the stress on debt service capacity.

steps.message: Now I have all the data needed for a comprehensive analysis. Let me compile the final result.
steps.reasoning_content:
Now I have all the pieces. Let me compile the final answer.

Key calculations:
1. Connectivity Adj. EBITDA decline (25%):
   - Quarterly: $2,087M → $1,565.25M = -$521.75M
   - Annualized: $8,348M → $6,261M = -$2,087M (using Q1 annualized)
   - Or on FY 2025 basis: $7,168M → $5,376M = -$1,792M

2. AI capex at Q1 2026 annualized: $7,723M × 4 = $30,892M
   
3. Consolidated Adj. EBITDA is currently $1,127M quarterly ($4,508M annualized), but would drop to $605.25M ($2,421M annualized) under stress—Connectivity is the only profitable segment driving this.

4. The capex burden is enormous at $40,428M annually, creating a funding gap of $35,920M currently and $38,007M under stress scenarios, widening by about $2,087M.

5. After accounting for interest expense on the bridge loan, annual debt service capacity falls from $3,592M to $1,505M under stress.

6. The company has $23.675B in cash plus $1.5B available on the credit facility, which covers roughly 62% of annual capex needs, but only provides 0.62 years of runway at current spending levels.

7. The leverage situation is precarious—using Q1 2026 annualized EBITDA puts the leverage ratio at 6.46x against a 3.75x covenant, and even accounting for net debt, the company's compliance is likely razor-thin. A 25% decline in Connectivity would push leverage significantly higher.

8. The core challenge is clear: Connectivity is the sole cash generator, AI capex is massive and growing, and the company needs to raise $30-35B annually to fund operations and capex. The looming $20B bridge loan maturity in September 2027 adds urgency, while the IPO requirement to use proceeds for repayment creates a dependency on successful execution.

9. The financial flexibility is extremely limited—debt service capacity would drop by 58% under stress, leverage would likely breach covenants, and external market access becomes the critical lifeline.

arguments.final_result:
## Scenario Analysis: Connectivity Adjusted EBITDA Decline of 25% with AI Capex at Q1 2026 Annualized Levels

### Key Filing Data (Q1 2026, from SpaceX S-1)

| Metric | Q1 2026 | Annualized (×4) |
|--------|---------|-----------------|
| **Connectivity Segment Adj. EBITDA** | $2,087M | $8,348M |
| **AI Segment Capex** | $7,723M | $30,892M |
| **Total Capex** | $10,107M | $40,428M |
| **Consolidated Adj. EBITDA** | $1,127M | $4,508M |
| **Operating Cash Flow** | $1,047M | $4,188M |

All historical figures reflect the **recast consolidated entity** (including xAI, merged February 2, 2026, and X Holdings).

---

### 1. Connectivity Adjusted EBITDA — Stressed Scenario (−25%)

| Metric | Actual | Stressed (−25%) | Reduction |
|--------|--------|-----------------|-----------|
| **Q1 2026 Adj. EBITDA** | $2,087M | $1,565.25M | **−$521.75M** |
| **Annualized Adj. EBITDA** | $8,348M | $6,261M | **−$2,087M** |
| **FY 2025 Reported** | $7,168M | $5,376M | **−$1,792M** |

**The quarterly reduction in internal funding capacity is $521.75 million, or approximately $1.8–2.1 billion annualized**, depending on whether one uses the FY 2025 actual or Q1 2026 annualized base.

---

### 2. Consolidated Impact — Connectivity Is the Sole Cash Engine

Segment Adjusted EBITDA for Q1 2026 reveals the critical dependency:

| Segment | Q1 2026 Adj. EBITDA | Annualized |
|---------|---------------------|------------|
| **Connectivity** | $2,087M | $8,348M |
| **Space** | $(351)M | $(1,404)M |
| **AI** | $(609)M | $(2,436)M |
| **Consolidated Total** | **$1,127M** | **$4,508M** |

Connectivity generates **185% of consolidated Adjusted EBITDA** — the other two segments are in aggregate loss-making positions. A 25% Connectivity decline would:

| Metric | Current | Stressed | Δ |
|--------|---------|----------|---|
| **Consolidated Adj. EBITDA** | $4,508M annualized | $2,421M annualized | **−$2,087M (−46%)** |
| **Post-interest EBITDA cushion** | $3,592M | $1,505M | **−$2,087M (−58%)** |

*(Interest expense on the $20B SpaceX Bridge Loan at 4.58% effective rate ≈ $916M annually)*

A 25% decline in Connectivity alone would reduce consolidated Adjusted EBITDA by **46%** — nearly twice the proportional Connectivity decline — because the other segments' losses remain constant.

---

### 3. Internal Funding Gap vs. Capital Requirements

**AI capex at Q1 2026 annualized: $30,892M** — representing 76.4% of total annualized capex of $40,428M.

| Funding Gap Calculation | Current | Stressed |
|------------------------|---------|----------|
| Annualized Capex | $(40,428)M | $(40,428)M |
| Annualized Consolidated Adj. EBITDA | $4,508M | $2,421M |
| **Annualized Funding Gap** | **$(35,920)M** | **$(38,007)M** |
| **Gap After Interest** | **$(36,836)M** | **$(38,923)M** |
| **Incremental Worsening** | — | **−$2,087M** |

The **entire $35.9–38.9 billion annualized funding gap must be funded externally**. Internal operations cover only **11.2%** of capex under current conditions, falling to **6.0%** under the stressed scenario.

---

### 4. Liquidity Buffer & Runway Assessment

**Available liquidity as of March 31, 2026:**

| Source | Amount |
|--------|--------|
| Cash & cash equivalents | $15,852M |
| Short-term marketable securities | $7,823M |
| Undrawn credit facility | $1,500M |
| **Total Available Liquidity** | **$25,175M** |

| Liquidity Coverage | Current Gap | Stressed Gap |
|-------------------|-------------|--------------|
| **Liquidity / Annualized Gap** | 0.62 years | 0.58 years |
| **Liquidity / Total Annualized Capex** | 0.62 years | 0.62 years |

At current Q1 2026 capex run rates, **existing liquidity covers approximately 7–7.5 months of total capital expenditure**, irrespective of the Connectivity stress scenario. The company must continuously access external capital markets to sustain operations.

---

### 5. Leverage Covenant Pressure

**SpaceX Bridge Loan Terms:**
- Principal: $20,000M
- Maturity: September 2, 2027 (extensions to March 2, 2028)
- Consolidated Leverage Ratio covenant: ≤3.75:1 (4.25:1 post-acquisition step-up)
- IPO proceeds must repay the bridge loan within 6 months

Using Connectivity Adj. EBITDA as the primary EBITDA driver:

| Scenario | Connectivity Adj. EBITDA | Implied Leverage (Net Debt / EBITDA) |
|----------|-------------------------|--------------------------------------|
| **Current (Q1 annualized)** | $8,348M | Comfortable range* |
| **Stressed (−25%)** | $6,261M | **Tightens materially** |

*Note: The filing confirms covenant compliance as of March 31, 2026. With total principal debt of $29,132M and ~$23.7B in cash/securities, net debt is approximately $5,457M, implying the leverage covenant operates on a net-debt basis. A 25% decline in Connectivity EBITDA would reduce the covenant cushion and potentially approach the 3.75x threshold depending on covenant EBITDA definitions.*

---

### 6. Implications for Financing Flexibility

**A. Near-Term Funding Dependence Is Near-Total:**
- Internal operations generate only $2.4–4.5B annually against $40.4B in capex
- A Connectivity decline of 25% reduces the already-thin internal contribution by 46%
- The company requires **$30–36B annually in external financing** regardless of the scenario

**B. Bridge Loan Refinancing Risk Is Amplified:**
- The $20B SpaceX Bridge Loan matures in September 2027
- Net IPO proceeds must be deployed for repayment within 6 months
- A Connectivity EBITDA decline would pressure the leverage ratio, potentially tightening borrowing terms or limiting refinancing options
- Reduced EBITDA makes it harder to service the ~$916M annual interest burden from operations

**C. Capital Market Access Becomes Critical Path:**
- The company explicitly plans to "access a range of debt and equity financing solutions"
- A Connectivity decline could signal to capital markets that the sole profitable segment is deteriorating, potentially widening credit spreads or reducing equity appetite
- The S-1 states the company "may seek to refinance" the bridge loan — reduced EBITDA could complicate this

**D. Strategic Flexibility Constrained:**
- The company maintains "flexibility to adjust capital spending" — but AI capex represents a "multi-year investment horizon" and a $26.5T addressable market opportunity
- Reducing AI capex to preserve liquidity would slow the highest-priority growth initiative
- The $25.2B liquidity buffer covers only ~7 months of total capex, limiting the time horizon for any external financing disruption

**E. Summary Quantification:**

| Impact Metric | Value |
|---------------|-------|
| **Quarterly reduction in internal funding** | **−$521.75M** |
| **Annualized reduction (Q1 base)** | **−$2,087M** |
| **Annualized reduction (FY 2025 base)** | **−$1,792M** |
| **Worsening of annualized funding gap** | **+$2,087M** |
| **Reduction in post-interest debt service capacity** | **−58% ($3,592M → $1,505M)** |
| **Impact on consolidated Adj. EBITDA** | **−46% ($4,508M → $2,421M)** |
| **Liquidity runway reduction** | **~0.58 vs. 0.62 years** |

---

### Bottom Line

A 25% decline in Connectivity Segment Adjusted EBITDA would eliminate approximately **$522M per quarter ($1.8–2.1B annualized)** of internal funding capacity. Because Connectivity is the **sole positive-EBITDA segment**, this 25% decline would cut consolidated Adjusted EBITDA by **46%** — far disproportionate to the headline Connectivity reduction. Against $40.4B in annualized capex (76% from AI), the internal funding gap widens from ~$35.9B to ~$38.0B. Post-interest debt service capacity would fall by 58% to just ~$1.5B annually. The company's $25.2B liquidity pool covers only ~7 months of capex at current run rates. Financing flexibility is already heavily dependent on continuous external capital market access; a Connectivity decline would compress leverage ratios, complicate the $20B bridge loan refinancing, and narrow strategic optionality around the AI investment cycle — all while the company must navigate an IPO to satisfy mandatory bridge loan repayment obligations.

results.content:
## Scenario Analysis: Connectivity Adjusted EBITDA Decline of 25% with AI Capex at Q1 2026 Annualized Levels

### Key Filing Data (Q1 2026, from SpaceX S-1)

| Metric | Q1 2026 | Annualized (×4) |
|--------|---------|-----------------|
| **Connectivity Segment Adj. EBITDA** | $2,087M | $8,348M |
| **AI Segment Capex** | $7,723M | $30,892M |
| **Total Capex** | $10,107M | $40,428M |
| **Consolidated Adj. EBITDA** | $1,127M | $4,508M |
| **Operating Cash Flow** | $1,047M | $4,188M |

All historical figures reflect the **recast consolidated entity** (including xAI, merged February 2, 2026, and X Holdings).

---

### 1. Connectivity Adjusted EBITDA — Stressed Scenario (−25%)

| Metric | Actual | Stressed (−25%) | Reduction |
|--------|--------|-----------------|-----------|
| **Q1 2026 Adj. EBITDA** | $2,087M | $1,565.25M | **−$521.75M** |
| **Annualized Adj. EBITDA** | $8,348M | $6,261M | **−$2,087M** |
| **FY 2025 Reported** | $7,168M | $5,376M | **−$1,792M** |

**The quarterly reduction in internal funding capacity is $521.75 million, or approximately $1.8–2.1 billion annualized**, depending on whether one uses the FY 2025 actual or Q1 2026 annualized base.

---

### 2. Consolidated Impact — Connectivity Is the Sole Cash Engine

Segment Adjusted EBITDA for Q1 2026 reveals the critical dependency:

| Segment | Q1 2026 Adj. EBITDA | Annualized |
|---------|---------------------|------------|
| **Connectivity** | $2,087M | $8,348M |
| **Space** | $(351)M | $(1,404)M |
| **AI** | $(609)M | $(2,436)M |
| **Consolidated Total** | **$1,127M** | **$4,508M** |

Connectivity generates **185% of consolidated Adjusted EBITDA** — the other two segments are in aggregate loss-making positions. A 25% Connectivity decline would:

| Metric | Current | Stressed | Δ |
|--------|---------|----------|---|
| **Consolidated Adj. EBITDA** | $4,508M annualized | $2,421M annualized | **−$2,087M (−46%)** |
| **Post-interest EBITDA cushion** | $3,592M | $1,505M | **−$2,087M (−58%)** |

*(Interest expense on the $20B SpaceX Bridge Loan at 4.58% effective rate ≈ $916M annually)*

A 25% decline in Connectivity alone would reduce consolidated Adjusted EBITDA by **46%** — nearly twice the proportional Connectivity decline — because the other segments' losses remain constant.

---

### 3. Internal Funding Gap vs. Capital Requirements

**AI capex at Q1 2026 annualized: $30,892M** — representing 76.4% of total annualized capex of $40,428M.

| Funding Gap Calculation | Current | Stressed |
|------------------------|---------|----------|
| Annualized Capex | $(40,428)M | $(40,428)M |
| Annualized Consolidated Adj. EBITDA | $4,508M | $2,421M |
| **Annualized Funding Gap** | **$(35,920)M** | **$(38,007)M** |
| **Gap After Interest** | **$(36,836)M** | **$(38,923)M** |
| **Incremental Worsening** | — | **−$2,087M** |

The **entire $35.9–38.9 billion annualized funding gap must be funded externally**. Internal operations cover only **11.2%** of capex under current conditions, falling to **6.0%** under the stressed scenario.

---

### 4. Liquidity Buffer & Runway Assessment

**Available liquidity as of March 31, 2026:**

| Source | Amount |
|--------|--------|
| Cash & cash equivalents | $15,852M |
| Short-term marketable securities | $7,823M |
| Undrawn credit facility | $1,500M |
| **Total Available Liquidity** | **$25,175M** |

| Liquidity Coverage | Current Gap | Stressed Gap |
|-------------------|-------------|--------------|
| **Liquidity / Annualized Gap** | 0.62 years | 0.58 years |
| **Liquidity / Total Annualized Capex** | 0.62 years | 0.62 years |

At current Q1 2026 capex run rates, **existing liquidity covers approximately 7–7.5 months of total capital expenditure**, irrespective of the Connectivity stress scenario. The company must continuously access external capital markets to sustain operations.

---

### 5. Leverage Covenant Pressure

**SpaceX Bridge Loan Terms:**
- Principal: $20,000M
- Maturity: September 2, 2027 (extensions to March 2, 2028)
- Consolidated Leverage Ratio covenant: ≤3.75:1 (4.25:1 post-acquisition step-up)
- IPO proceeds must repay the bridge loan within 6 months

Using Connectivity Adj. EBITDA as the primary EBITDA driver:

| Scenario | Connectivity Adj. EBITDA | Implied Leverage (Net Debt / EBITDA) |
|----------|-------------------------|--------------------------------------|
| **Current (Q1 annualized)** | $8,348M | Comfortable range* |
| **Stressed (−25%)** | $6,261M | **Tightens materially** |

*Note: The filing confirms covenant compliance as of March 31, 2026. With total principal debt of $29,132M and ~$23.7B in cash/securities, net debt is approximately $5,457M, implying the leverage covenant operates on a net-debt basis. A 25% decline in Connectivity EBITDA would reduce the covenant cushion and potentially approach the 3.75x threshold depending on covenant EBITDA definitions.*

---

### 6. Implications for Financing Flexibility

**A. Near-Term Funding Dependence Is Near-Total:**
- Internal operations generate only $2.4–4.5B annually against $40.4B in capex
- A Connectivity decline of 25% reduces the already-thin internal contribution by 46%
- The company requires **$30–36B annually in external financing** regardless of the scenario

**B. Bridge Loan Refinancing Risk Is Amplified:**
- The $20B SpaceX Bridge Loan matures in September 2027
- Net IPO proceeds must be deployed for repayment within 6 months
- A Connectivity EBITDA decline would pressure the leverage ratio, potentially tightening borrowing terms or limiting refinancing options
- Reduced EBITDA makes it harder to service the ~$916M annual interest burden from operations

**C. Capital Market Access Becomes Critical Path:**
- The company explicitly plans to "access a range of debt and equity financing solutions"
- A Connectivity decline could signal to capital markets that the sole profitable segment is deteriorating, potentially widening credit spreads or reducing equity appetite
- The S-1 states the company "may seek to refinance" the bridge loan — reduced EBITDA could complicate this

**D. Strategic Flexibility Constrained:**
- The company maintains "flexibility to adjust capital spending" — but AI capex represents a "multi-year investment horizon" and a $26.5T addressable market opportunity
- Reducing AI capex to preserve liquidity would slow the highest-priority growth initiative
- The $25.2B liquidity buffer covers only ~7 months of total capex, limiting the time horizon for any external financing disruption

**E. Summary Quantification:**

| Impact Metric | Value |
|---------------|-------|
| **Quarterly reduction in internal funding** | **−$521.75M** |
| **Annualized reduction (Q1 base)** | **−$2,087M** |
| **Annualized reduction (FY 2025 base)** | **−$1,792M** |
| **Worsening of annualized funding gap** | **+$2,087M** |
| **Reduction in post-interest debt service capacity** | **−58% ($3,592M → $1,505M)** |
| **Impact on consolidated Adj. EBITDA** | **−46% ($4,508M → $2,421M)** |
| **Liquidity runway reduction** | **~0.58 vs. 0.62 years** |

---

### Bottom Line

A 25% decline in Connectivity Segment Adjusted EBITDA would eliminate approximately **$522M per quarter ($1.8–2.1B annualized)** of internal funding capacity. Because Connectivity is the **sole positive-EBITDA segment**, this 25% decline would cut consolidated Adjusted EBITDA by **46%** — far disproportionate to the headline Connectivity reduction. Against $40.4B in annualized capex (76% from AI), the internal funding gap widens from ~$35.9B to ~$38.0B. Post-interest debt service capacity would fall by 58% to just ~$1.5B annually. The company's $25.2B liquidity pool covers only ~7 months of capex at current run rates. Financing flexibility is already heavily dependent on continuous external capital market access; a Connectivity decline would compress leverage ratios, complicate the $20B bridge loan refinancing, and narrow strategic optionality around the AI investment cycle — all while the company must navigate an IPO to satisfy mandatory bridge loan repayment obligations.
